auth_prerequisites: Model or provider credentials are required for realistic end-to-end coding sessions.

non_interactive_execution.summary: Public docs and the published PyPI package show a documented CLI install and execution surface.
non_interactive_execution.notes: The terminal entrypoint is publicly documented and packaged.

offline_strategy.summary: Wrapper-level tests can rely on fixture capture or a fake aider binary before any live provider session.
offline_strategy.notes: The recommendation lane only needs deterministic parser and wrapper proof.

observable_cli_surface.summary: The docs and repo describe a terminal-facing tool with observable CLI behavior.
observable_cli_surface.notes: Observable CLI surface is documented publicly.

redaction_fit.summary: CLI stdout and stderr are text streams that can be sanitized before persistence.
redaction_fit.notes: Redaction fit is a repo-local inference from terminal-oriented IO.

crate_first_fit.summary: Aider ships as an external installable CLI, which aligns with wrapper-crate-first onboarding.
crate_first_fit.notes: The wrapper can own process invocation before any deeper adapter work.

reproducibility.summary: Public install docs and PyPI distribution make local setup repeatable before provider auth is added.
reproducibility.notes: Repeatable local setup is public, while provider sessions remain gated later.

future_leverage.summary: Aider’s adoption and active packaging make it a strong candidate for future repo leverage.
future_leverage.notes: Upstream adoption is strong enough to treat aider as a serious finalist.

blocked_steps: Provider-backed coding sessions still need maintainer credentials after local CLI install.
normalized_caveats: Public docs cover local install now; provider-backed coding behavior should be proven in onboarding.

evidence.title: Aider - AI Pair Programming in Your Terminal
evidence.excerpt: Aider docs say "AI pair programming in your terminal" and present public getting-started, documentation, and install-oriented surfaces.
